critic_raw:
The stratified random split across 'regions and crop types' explicitly dismisses temporal dependencies based on domain knowledge suggesting 'weak temporal autocorrelation over the selected period.' But crop yield prediction from satellite imagery has a strong seasonal structure (NDVI peaks, growing degree days, precipitation timing) that is temporally correlated across years in the same region. The critical confound is not autocorrelation between years—it is that the same region in the same season always looks similar. Random splitting allows the CNN to train on images of the same field in different years while testing on the same field in a held-out year, which tests spatial memorization rather than generalization to new agricultural areas or new growing conditions.

PCA is applied to the spectral features 'fitted exclusively on training data.' For multispectral data with typically 6-12 spectral bands, PCA is applied across all pixels in all training images. This produces a principal component space defined by the training imagery. When test-set images (from potentially different growing seasons, cloud conditions, or sensor calibration epochs) are projected into this space, the projections may not be comparable if the test images have different spectral statistics than the training images. Atmospheric correction and inter-seasonal radiometric normalization are not described as preprocessing steps, which is a significant gap for multi-year MODIS data.

The CNN operates on 'spectral data' from MODIS, which has a spatial resolution of 250m-500m per pixel. At this resolution, each pixel is a mixture of multiple land cover types and crop varieties. Treating a 500m MODIS pixel as a homogeneous agricultural unit ignores sub-pixel heterogeneity that determines yield variability. The design does not describe whether the CNN operates on individual pixels or on spatial windows, and the spatial granularity of the crop yield ground truth (typically reported at county or administrative level, not pixel level) creates a spatial mismatch between the input resolution and the label resolution.

The linear regression baseline uses 'historical agricultural data' rather than the same satellite features. This is not a fair comparison of model architectures—it compares a satellite-based feature set with a historical-statistics-based feature set. A fair baseline would apply linear regression to the same MODIS spectral features the CNN uses, to isolate whether the CNN's nonlinear spatial feature extraction adds value over linear regression on identical inputs.

MAE in tonnes per hectare is reported as the primary metric without context about the range of crop yields. If corn yields vary from 3 to 12 t/ha in the dataset, a 1 t/ha MAE is a 10-30% relative error; if wheat yields vary from 1 to 5 t/ha, the same absolute MAE represents a 20-100% relative error. Without normalizing by yield range (using MAPE or coefficient of variation of error), MAE cannot be compared across crop types or interpreted in a meaningful practical context.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split allows the CNN to train on images of the same agricultural field in different years while testing on the same field in a held-out year; this tests spatial memorization of field-specific features rather than generalization to new regions.

PCA on multi-year MODIS data without atmospheric correction or radiometric normalization produces a PC space dominated by inter-annual spectral variation from calibration drift and atmospheric effects rather than land cover and yield-related features.

MODIS spatial resolution (250m-500m) creates sub-pixel heterogeneity that is not addressed; treating a mixed-land-cover pixel as a homogeneous agricultural unit, and the spatial mismatch between pixel-level input and county-level label resolution, are unaddressed.

The linear regression baseline uses historical agricultural statistics rather than the same satellite features as the CNN; the comparison tests feature set differences rather than model architecture differences.

MAE in tonnes per hectare is reported without normalization across crop types with different yield ranges; absolute MAE is not comparable across crops and cannot be interpreted practically without reference to yield distribution.
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split allows the CNN to train on images of the same agricultural field in different years while testing on the same field in a held-out year; this tests spatial memorization of field-specific features rather than generalization to new regions.

PCA on multi-year MODIS data without atmospheric correction or radiometric normalization produces a PC space dominated by inter-annual spectral variation from calibration drift and atmospheric effects rather than land cover and yield-related features.

MODIS spatial resolution (250m-500m) creates sub-pixel heterogeneity that is not addressed; treating a mixed-land-cover pixel as a homogeneous agricultural unit, and the spatial mismatch between pixel-level input and county-level label resolution, are unaddressed.

The linear regression baseline uses historical agricultural statistics rather than the same satellite features as the CNN; the comparison tests feature set differences rather than model architecture differences.

MAE in tonnes per hectare is reported without normalization across crop types with different yield ranges; absolute MAE is not comparable across crops and cannot be interpreted practically without reference to yield distribution.
